title: Depth, Width, and Resolution
concept: EfficientNet focuses on three main ways a CNN can grow: depth adds more layers of processing, width adds more channels per layer, and resolution feeds the model more detailed input images.

exercises.prompt: Recap: EfficientNet's balanced scaling combines which three model-growth axes?

options.text: Kernel color, font size, and batch order

exercises.explain: Correct — now let's make each of those three axes concrete.

cards.title: Three knobs, three different effects
cards.body: When a CNN gets bigger, not all growth is the same. Adding more layers changes how long the processing chain is. Adding more channels changes how much feature space each layer has. Increasing image size changes how much raw detail enters the network at the start.

cards.title: The three scaling axes
cards.body: Depth means more stacked layers or blocks. Width means more channels or feature maps inside layers. Resolution means feeding the network larger input images, such as going from 160×160 to 224×224.

cards.title: Longer line, wider line, sharper parts
cards.body: Imagine a factory. Depth is adding more stations in sequence. Width is adding more workers at each station. Resolution is receiving parts with finer markings and detail. Each change helps differently, so you need to know what knob you are turning.

cards.title: Bird classifier example
cards.body: If your bird classifier misses tiny feather patterns, higher resolution may help because the input image carries more detail. If the network sees the detail but cannot express enough feature types, more width may help. If it needs more stages of reasoning to combine patterns, more depth may help.

cards.title: These axes are related, but not identical
cards.body: People sometimes talk about a model becoming 'bigger' as if that were one thing. But a deeper model, a wider model, and a higher-resolution model can behave very differently. EfficientNet matters partly because it separates these axes instead of hiding them under one vague word: bigger.

cards.title: What each axis changes
cards.body: Each knob grows a different part of the system.

left.text:
More layers or blocks
Longer processing chain
More sequential reasoning

right.text:
More channels per layer
More detail in the input image
Broader features + finer visual information

exercises.prompt: What does scaling width change most directly in a CNN?

options.text: The number of channels or feature maps available in layers
The file format of the input images

exercises.explain: Exactly — width is about channel capacity inside the network.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Increasing resolution means the CNN receives a more detailed input image.
exercises.explain: True — higher resolution can preserve smaller visual details at the input.

exercises.sentence: Adding more layers mainly scales a model's ___.
exercises.explain: Right — depth refers to how many processing stages are stacked in sequence.

exercises.prompt: Which diagnosis uses the scaling axes correctly?

options.text: If tiny defects disappear after image downsampling, resolution may be the first knob to inspect
If a network needs more feature channels, you should rename depth to width and hope it works
If a model is too shallow, the best fix is always to lower image resolution

exercises.explain: Yes — the axes help you reason about which part of the system is likely limiting performance.

exercises.prompt: A CNN already sees detailed images, but each layer has very few channels and cannot represent many feature types. Which scaling axis is the most direct match for that problem?

options.text: Width, because more channels expand feature capacity within each layer
Resolution, because the issue is not feature capacity but only image file size
Depth, because adding any number of layers automatically increases channels
None of them, because CNN scaling has nothing to do with channels

exercises.explain: Exactly. Depth, width, and resolution solve different limits. Next, we will see why scaling only one axis can leave another part of the network as the bottleneck.
